text: A 77-year-old man with metastatic prostate cancer reports worsening back pain despite taking 60 mg of morphine daily. He asks for medications at exactly the prescribed times and is very irritable when demanding breakthrough doses. Which is the most likely explanation?
rationale: Dx: Undertreated pain. Patients with chronic cancer pain often display behaviors misinterpreted as 'drug-seeking', such as irritability or demanding timely doses, when their pain is not adequately controlled[span_0](end_span). [span_1](start_span)Tx: Reassess and increase the analgesic dose[span_1](end_span).

text: A 21-year-old man with a history of seizures is brought to the ED with a generalized tonic-clonic seizure. He is unresponsive with twitching of the mouth and both arms. Fingerstick glucose is 6.3 mmol/L. What is the most appropriate first-line pharmacotherapy?
rationale: Dx: Generalized convulsive status epilepticus (GCSE), defined as a seizure lasting >5 minutes[span_2](end_span)[span_3](end_span). [span_4](start_span)[span_5](start_span)Tx: Benzodiazepines (e.g., lorazepam) are the first-line therapy to terminate seizure activity[span_4](end_span)[span_5](end_span).

text: A 4-year-old boy presents with fever, sore throat, and neck stiffness. He is unable to fully open his mouth (trismus) and cannot fully extend his neck due to pain. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Dx: Retropharyngeal abscess (RPA). [span_6](start_span)Classic symptoms include fever, odynophagia, neck stiffness (especially with extension), and trismus[span_6](end_span). [span_7](start_span)RPA classically occurs in children aged 2-4 after a respiratory infection[span_7](end_span).

text: A 56-year-old man with a history of IV drug use presents with an episodic rash (palpable purpura), arthralgia, Raynaud phenomenon, and tingling feet. Labs show RF positive, low C4, and positive cryoglobulins. What is the most appropriate next test?
rationale: Dx: Cryoglobulinemic vasculitis. [span_8](start_span)90% of cases are associated with Hepatitis C virus infection[span_8](end_span). [span_9](start_span)Tx: Treating the underlying Hepatitis C will also treat the vasculitis[span_9](end_span).

text: A 30-year-old woman with bipolar disorder taking lithium confirms she is pregnant. She is concerned about the effects of lithium on the fetus. What should you tell her regarding the risk?
rationale: Dx: Lithium use in pregnancy increases the risk of cardiac abnormalities, specifically Ebstein anomaly[span_10](end_span)[span_11](end_span). [span_12](start_span)Note: Valproic acid is more commonly associated with neural tube defects[span_12](end_span).

text: A 25-year-old female reports excessive worry about an upcoming work presentation and office party, fearing she will embarrass herself or be scrutinized by others. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Dx: Social anxiety disorder (Social phobia). [span_13](start_span)[span_14](start_span)Characterized by a persistent fear of social or performance situations resulting in humiliation or scrutiny by others[span_13](end_span)[span_14](end_span).

text: Regarding the 25-year-old female diagnosed with social anxiety disorder, what is the recommended first-line treatment?
rationale: Tx: First-line therapy for social anxiety disorder is cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT), including exposure therapy[span_15](end_span). [span_16](start_span)[span_17](start_span)Second-line treatments include SSRIs or beta-blockers[span_16](end_span)[span_17](end_span).

text: A 64-year-old man receiving IV prochlorperazine for chemotherapy-induced nausea feels very restless and cannot stop moving his legs. Which medication could have prevented this reaction?
rationale: Dx: Akathisia (restlessness) caused by neuroleptic medications like prochlorperazine[span_18](end_span)[span_19](end_span). [span_20](start_span)[span_21](start_span)Prevention: Most reliably maintained by using a benzodiazepine like lorazepam[span_20](end_span)[span_21](end_span).

text: A 72-year-old woman with stable angina has a carotid bruit. Duplex shows complete occlusion of the right ICA and 30% stenosis of the left ICA. She has no neurological symptoms. Best management?
rationale: Tx: Medical therapy only (aspirin, statin, BP control)[span_22](end_span). [span_23](start_span)[span_24](start_span)CEA is not indicated for complete occlusion of the internal carotid artery or for mild asymptomatic stenosis[span_23](end_span)[span_24](end_span).

text: A 42-year-old obese man (BMI 33) is being treated for hypertension. Which of the following medications is associated with the highest risk of developing diabetes mellitus?
rationale: Dx: Thiazide diuretics (e.g., Chlorthalidone) have the greatest effect on serum glucose and increase the risk of developing diabetes[span_25](end_span). [span_26](start_span)[span_27](start_span)Note: Carvedilol is an exception among beta-blockers and does not increase DM risk[span_26](end_span)[span_27](end_span).

text: A screening test used in the general population and a confirmatory diagnostic test have the same sensitivity, but the diagnostic test has a substantially higher positive predictive value (PPV). Why?
rationale: Dx: Positive Predictive Value (PPV) is directly related to disease prevalence[span_28](end_span). [span_29](start_span)Since the diagnostic test is applied to a population with suspected disease (higher prevalence), its PPV is naturally higher[span_29](end_span).

text: A 34-year-old woman smokes one pack per day and has a family history of rheumatoid arthritis (RA). Which of the following is most likely to reduce her risk of developing RA?
rationale: Dx: Smoking is a provocative environmental factor for seropositive RA, causing lung inflammation and citrullination of peptides[span_30](end_span)[span_31](end_span)[span_32](end_span). [span_33](start_span)Tx: Smoking cessation decreases this risk[span_33](end_span).

text: A 58-year-old man with a 20-year history of GERD is a smoker and is obese (BMI 37). His symptoms are well-controlled on omeprazole. What is the most appropriate initial management for his GERD?
rationale: Dx: Upper endoscopy is indicated to screen for Barrett esophagus in men >50 with long-standing GERD (>5 years) and multiple risk factors (obesity, tobacco use)[span_34](end_span)[span_35](end_span).

text: A 42-year-old woman has trouble sleeping, weight loss, and re-experiences a motor vehicle accident that occurred 6 months ago. Which symptom helps distinguish PTSD from Acute Stress Disorder?
rationale: Dx: PTSD requires a duration of symptoms >1 month[span_36](end_span). [span_37](start_span)Acute stress disorder lasts less than 4 weeks[span_37](end_span). [span_38](start_span)[span_39](start_span)Re-experiencing and sleep issues occur in both[span_38](end_span)[span_39](end_span).

text: A 67-year-old man feels a 'pop' in his knee. Exam shows an inferiorly displaced patella (patella baja) and inability to extend the leg. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Dx: Quadriceps tendon rupture. [span_40](start_span)[span_41](start_span)Findings include inability to extend the knee and inferior patellar displacement (patella baja)[span_40](end_span)[span_41](end_span). [span_42](start_span)[span_43](start_span)Note: Patellar tendon rupture causes superior displacement (patella alta)[span_42](end_span)[span_43](end_span).

text: A 22-year-old female collapses after laughter (no loss of consciousness). She has a 4-month history of daytime sleepiness and vivid dreams upon falling asleep. Diagnosis?
rationale: Dx: Narcolepsy. [span_44](start_span)Characterized by cataplexy (loss of muscle tone with intense emotion), daytime sleepiness, and hypnagogic/hypnopompic hallucinations[span_44](end_span).

text: Regarding the 22-year-old female suspected of narcolepsy, what is the best next step in confirming the diagnosis?
rationale: Dx: Polysomnography (sleep study) can diagnose narcolepsy[span_45](end_span). [span_46](start_span)Characteristic findings include multiple spontaneous awakenings and reduced REM latency[span_46](end_span).

text: What is the most appropriate first-line pharmacotherapy for daytime somnolence in a patient with narcolepsy?
rationale: Tx: Modafinil is a non-amphetamine stimulant used as a first-line treatment for somnolence in narcolepsy[span_47](end_span)[span_48](end_span).

text: A 2-year-old boy presents with a large amount of painless, 'brick-red' rectal bleeding. Abdominal exam is normal. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Dx: Meckel diverticulum. [span_49](start_span)[span_50](start_span)Classically presents as painless, massive brick-red rectal bleeding in a child <2 years old (Rule of 2s)[span_49](end_span)[span_50](end_span). [span_51](start_span)Diagnosis is via technetium scan[span_51](end_span).

text: A 25-year-old woman with a history of epilepsy had a breakthrough seizure after stopping valproate. She is now restarted on medication. How long must she remain seizure-free before resuming driving?
rationale: Tx: Patients with epilepsy should be cautioned not to drive until a seizure-free period (typically 6-12 months) has occurred, regardless of medication status[span_52](end_span).
